questions.q: Begum Hazrat Mahal led the 1857 revolt in which kingdom?
questions.explanation: Begum Hazrat Mahal organized the revolt in Awadh (Oudh) during 1857.

questions.q: Begum Hazrat Mahal organized resistance during 1857 in which city?
questions.explanation: She led the resistance from Lucknow, the capital of Awadh.

questions.q: Begum Hazrat Mahal fought on behalf of her young son, who was proclaimed ruler. What was his name?
questions.explanation: She championed the cause of her son Birjis Qadr, proclaimed Wali (ruler) of Awadh.

questions.q: Where did Begum Hazrat Mahal take refuge after the rebellion failed?
questions.explanation: When the revolt collapsed she escaped to Nepal, where she spent her remaining years.

questions.q: In which year did Begum Hazrat Mahal die in exile?
questions.explanation: Begum Hazrat Mahal died in Nepal in 1879.

questions.q: Begum Hazrat Mahal was a wife of which deposed ruler of Awadh?
questions.explanation: She was a begum of Nawab Wajid Ali Shah, the last king of Awadh.

questions.q: The British action that fuelled the Awadh revolt led by Begum Hazrat Mahal was the annexation of which kingdom?
questions.explanation: The British annexation of Awadh in 1856 was a major grievance behind the revolt she led.

questions.q: Begum Hazrat Mahal is honoured as a leading woman figure of which event?
questions.explanation: She is remembered as one of the foremost women leaders of the Revolt of 1857.

questions.q: Begum Hazrat Mahal's revolt was directed against the rule of which power?
questions.explanation: Her struggle was directed against British rule following the annexation of Awadh.

questions.q: By the title 'Begum,' Hazrat Mahal held what kind of status?
questions.explanation: As a Begum she held the status of a royal consort in the court of Awadh.

questions.q: Begum Hazrat Mahal's husband Wajid Ali Shah was exiled by the British to which city?
questions.explanation: Wajid Ali Shah was deposed in 1856 and exiled to Calcutta (the Matiya Burj area).

questions.q: Begum Hazrat Mahal's son Birjis Qadr was proclaimed ruler of Awadh in which year?
questions.explanation: Birjis Qadr was proclaimed Wali of Awadh in 1857 during the revolt.

questions.q: Which fortified palace complex in Lucknow served as a centre of the rebel court under Begum Hazrat Mahal?
questions.explanation: The Qaisar Bagh palace in Lucknow was a centre of the rebel administration she helped lead.

questions.q: Begum Hazrat Mahal's original name before entering the royal household is often given as what?
questions.explanation: She is commonly said to have been named Muhammadi Khanum before joining the Awadh court.

questions.q: Begum Hazrat Mahal issued counter-proclamations rejecting which 1858 British royal proclamation?
questions.explanation: She issued a famous counter-proclamation challenging Queen Victoria's Proclamation of 1858.

questions.q: The British recaptured Lucknow from the rebels led by Begum Hazrat Mahal in which year?
questions.explanation: British forces under Sir Colin Campbell retook Lucknow in March 1858.

questions.q: Which Nepalese ruler granted Begum Hazrat Mahal refuge after the revolt?
questions.explanation: Prime Minister Jung Bahadur Rana of Nepal granted her asylum after 1857.

questions.q: In which city of Nepal did Begum Hazrat Mahal live in exile and eventually die?
questions.explanation: She lived out her exile in Kathmandu, where she died in 1879.

questions.q: Begum Hazrat Mahal worked alongside which Awadh rebel commander often called the 'Maulvi of Faizabad'?
questions.explanation: Maulvi Ahmadullah Shah of Faizabad was a prominent allied leader in the Awadh revolt.

questions.q: A noted commemorative park named after Begum Hazrat Mahal stands in which city today?
questions.explanation: The Begum Hazrat Mahal Park in Lucknow honours her role in the 1857 revolt.

questions.q: Awadh, the kingdom Begum Hazrat Mahal fought for, is located in which present-day Indian state?
questions.explanation: Awadh lies in present-day Uttar Pradesh, with Lucknow as its historic capital.

questions.q: Begum Hazrat Mahal's resistance is frequently compared with that of which other woman leader of 1857?
questions.explanation: She is often paired with Rani Lakshmibai of Jhansi as a leading woman of the 1857 revolt.

questions.q: The British annexed Awadh, sparking the grievances behind Begum Hazrat Mahal's revolt, in which year?
questions.explanation: Awadh was annexed by the British in 1856 under the pretext of misgovernment.

questions.q: Which British commander led the relief of the Lucknow Residency, besieged during the revolt associated with Begum Hazrat Mahal?
questions.explanation: Generals Havelock and Outram led the first relief of the besieged Lucknow Residency in 1857.

questions.q: Begum Hazrat Mahal rejected British amnesty terms partly over the issue of desecrated or seized religious sites; her son was proclaimed under which title?
questions.explanation: Her son Birjis Qadr was proclaimed Wali (ruler) of Awadh, and she rejected terms that ignored such grievances.

questions.q: The final British reconquest of Lucknow that ended Begum Hazrat Mahal's hold occurred in which month of 1858?
questions.explanation: Sir Colin Campbell's forces completed the recapture of Lucknow in March 1858.

questions.q: Begum Hazrat Mahal's grave lies in the grounds of which mosque in Kathmandu?
questions.explanation: She was buried in the grounds of the Jama Masjid in Kathmandu, Nepal.

questions.q: Birjis Qadr, son of Begum Hazrat Mahal, was allowed to return to India years later and died in which decade?
questions.explanation: Birjis Qadr eventually returned to India and died in 1893, in the 1890s.

questions.q: India issued a commemorative postage stamp honouring Begum Hazrat Mahal in which year?
questions.explanation: A postage stamp commemorating Begum Hazrat Mahal was issued by India in 1984.

questions.q: The annexation of Awadh that preceded Begum Hazrat Mahal's revolt was carried out under which Governor-General?
questions.explanation: Lord Dalhousie's administration annexed Awadh in 1856, shortly before he was succeeded by Lord Canning.
